---
title: "Protocols as Parameters"
id: sec-protocols-as-parameters
---

# Protocols as Parameters {#sec-protocols-as-parameters}

When a protocol combines multiple structural concerns, it merges their descriptions into one, identifying the parts they share. The result is a single coherent theory that the engine can reason about uniformly.

A *protocol* is a pair of theories: one for the shape of schemas, one for the shape of data. ATProto's schema theory is $\mathrm{colimit}(\text{ThGraph}, \text{ThConstraint}, \text{ThMulti})$. Its instance theory is $\text{ThWType}$. Migration, diffing, breaking-change detection, and bidirectional lenses are all derived from that pair.

panproto supports 76 protocols organized into six theory groups.


## The Two-Parameter Architecture

Every supported protocol is described by exactly two parameters:

1. **Schema theory** $\mathbb{T}_S$: the algebraic theory whose models are schemas. This determines what "a schema" looks like (vertices and edges, vertices and hyperedges, constraints, interfaces, or some combination).

2. **Instance theory** $\mathbb{T}_I$: the algebraic theory whose models are data instances conforming to a schema. This determines what "a record" or "a row" looks like once you have a schema in hand.

A protocol is thus a pair $\mathcal{P} = (\mathbb{T}_S, \mathbb{T}_I)$. The following table shows representative protocols from each theory group. The full catalog of all 76 supported protocols is in [Appendix C](../appendices/C-protocol-catalog.qmd).

The `Th` prefix means "theory of": `ThGraph` is the theory of directed graphs, `ThConstraint` is the theory of constraint-decorated structures, and so on. $\mathrm{colimit}(\text{ThGraph}, \text{ThConstraint}, \text{ThMulti})$ means "merge these three theories together, identifying their shared parts." The result is a single theory combining graph structure, constraints, and multiplicity (parallel edges).

| Protocol | Schema Theory | Instance Theory | Restrict |
|----------|--------------|----------------|----------|
| **Group A: Constrained Multigraph + W-Type** | | | |
| ATProto | colimit(ThGraph, ThConstraint, ThMulti) | ThWType + ThMeta | Sig. restriction + BFS + contraction |
| JSON Schema | colimit(ThGraph, ThConstraint, ThMulti) | ThWType | Sig. restriction + BFS + contraction |
| **Group B: Hypergraph + Set-Valued Map** | | | |
| SQL | colimit(ThHypergraph, ThConstraint) | ThFunctor | `restrict` (precomposition) |
| Parquet | colimit(ThHypergraph, ThConstraint) | ThFunctor | `restrict` (precomposition) |
| **Group C: Simple Graph + Flat** | | | |
| Protobuf | ThSimpleGraph + ThConstraint | ThFlat | Field projection |
| Avro | ThSimpleGraph + ThConstraint | ThFlat | Field projection |
| **Group D: Typed Graph + W-Type with Interfaces** | | | |
| GraphQL | colimit(ThGraph, ThConstraint, ThMulti, ThInterface) | ThWType | Sig. restriction + BFS + contraction |
| TypeScript | colimit(ThGraph, ThConstraint, ThMulti, ThInterface) | ThWType | Sig. restriction + BFS + contraction |
| **Group E: Constrained Multigraph + W-Type + Meta** | | | |
| XML/XSD | colimit(ThGraph, ThConstraint, ThMulti, ThMeta) | ThWType | Sig. restriction + BFS + contraction |
| Neo4j | colimit(ThGraph, ThConstraint, ThMulti, ThMeta) | ThWType | Sig. restriction + BFS + contraction |

: Representative protocols from each theory group. {#tbl-protocol-table}

Several patterns are visible:

- **Schema theories are composed from building blocks.** Real-world protocols combine several concerns: graphs with constraints, multigraph structure with interfaces. panproto assembles the schema theory by merging simpler component theories, identifying shared structure along the way.^[This merge operation is formally a *colimit* (pushout) in the category of theories. See [Appendix A](../appendices/A-formal-foundations.qmd).]

- **Instance theories cluster into three families.** Set-valued maps ($\text{ThFunctor}$), tree-shaped data ($\text{ThWType}$), and flat records ($\text{ThFlat}$). This split determines how migration works at the data level.

- **The restriction operator is determined by the instance theory.** Set-valued map instances migrate via `restrict` (precomposition). W-type instances migrate via the signature-restriction pipeline. Flat instances use field projection (the depth-1 specialization of tree surgery). You don't choose a migration strategy; it follows from the theory.


## Three Families of Instances

The instance theory determines what "data" means for a given schema, and therefore what "migrating data" means.

### Set-Valued Maps ($\text{ThFunctor}$) {#sec-functor-instances}

Think of a set-valued map instance as a filing cabinet: each table gets a drawer, each drawer holds a set of rows, and foreign keys are cross-references between drawers.

In the set-valued map model, a schema is a collection of tables (or types) connected by foreign keys (or relationships).^[Formally, an instance is a functor $I: \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Set}$, where $\mathcal{C}$ is the schema viewed as a category. See @spivak2012 and [Appendix A](../appendices/A-formal-foundations.qmd).] An *instance* of that schema assigns to each table a set of rows, and to each foreign key a lookup function mapping rows of the source table to rows of the target table. Referential integrity (following two foreign keys in sequence gives the same result whether you compose them first or chase them one at a time) is built into the structure.

**Migration** is `restrict`: given a schema morphism $f: S_1 \to S_2$ and an instance $I$ over $S_2$, you get an instance $f^* I$ over $S_1$ by looking up each old table's corresponding new table and pulling back its rows. Foreign keys compose automatically.^[In categorical language, $f^*$ is the pullback functor $\Delta_f$, with left adjoint `extend` ($\Sigma_f$) and right adjoint `complete` ($\Pi_f$).]

SQL databases, Confluent Schema Registry Avro schemas, Parquet files, and Arrow tables all fall into this family.

### W-Types: Tree-Shaped Data ($\text{ThWType}$) {#sec-wtype-instances}

Most protocols (ATProto, GraphQL, JSON Schema, TypeScript, and the majority of the 76 protocols panproto supports) traffic in *tree-shaped* data. A Bluesky post is not a row in a table; it is a JSON document with nested objects, arrays, and references.

A W-type (wellfounded tree type) instance over a schema is a finitely-branching tree where:^[The mathematical model is a W-type studied by @gambino2004 in the context of dependent type theory and related to the containers of @abbott2005. See [Appendix A](../appendices/A-formal-foundations.qmd).]

- Each node is anchored to a schema vertex. The root might be anchored to `Post`, a child to `Text`, a sibling to `CreatedAt`.
- Each branch is labeled by a schema edge.
- The tree is wellfounded: every path from root to leaf is finite.

**Migration** runs a five-step pipeline: (1) signature restriction, (2) reachability BFS, (3) ancestor contraction, (4) edge resolution, (5) fan reconstruction. @sec-lifting-data covers this pipeline in full detail.

### Flat Instances: Depth-1 Trees ($\text{ThFlat}$) {#sec-flat-instances}

$\text{ThFlat}$ is a disciplined restriction of $\text{ThWType}$. When the schema is a simple graph (no parallel edges, no recursive types) and instances are constrained to depth-1 trees, the full W-type machinery reduces to *field projection*: keep the fields whose schema edges appear in the image of the migration map; discard the rest.

This captures a genuine semantic boundary. Protocols like Protobuf, Avro, and Thrift are deliberately flat, designed for high-throughput serialization where recursive nesting would impose unacceptable overhead. Treating $\text{ThFlat}$ as a proper theory ensures that panproto enforces the depth-1 constraint at the theory level.

$\text{ThFlat}$ is the depth-1 case of $\text{ThWType}$: every flat record is trivially a tree with no children, so any $\text{ThFlat}$ instance is automatically a valid $\text{ThWType}$ instance. The converse does not hold.

::: {.callout-caution}
## Question

Protobuf uses $\text{ThFlat}$ for its instance theory. Why not $\text{ThWType}$? Protobuf messages *can* contain nested messages. What structural property of Protobuf makes $\text{ThFlat}$ sufficient?
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse=true}
## Answer

Protobuf messages *can* contain nested messages, but each nested message is a separately addressable, independently encoded unit with its own field numbers. The nesting is a reference, not a recursive tree structure. A Protobuf message is fully described by its immediate fields, making it depth-1 by design. $\text{ThFlat}$ captures this: migration reduces to field projection, which is cheaper than the full W-type tree surgery pipeline.
:::

## Comparing the Three Models

| Aspect | Set-Valued Map ($\text{ThFunctor}$) | W-Type ($\text{ThWType}$) | Flat ($\text{ThFlat}$) |
|--------|---------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| Data shape | Tables of rows | Nested trees of nodes | Depth-1 key-value records |
| Schema role | Objects + morphisms | Graph (vertices + edges) | Simple graph (no parallel edges) |
| Migration | `restrict` (precomposition) | Tree surgery pipeline | Field projection |
| Canonical users | SQL, Parquet, Arrow | ATProto, GraphQL, TypeScript | Protobuf, Avro, Thrift |
| Compositionality | Automatic | Requires reachability analysis | Trivial (projection) |

: Comparing the three instance theory families. {#tbl-instance-comparison}

No model is universally better. Set-valued maps have superior compositional properties (migration maps compose, and `extend`/`complete` give you round-tripping for free), but demand tabular data. W-types handle trees natively, at the cost of more complex migration logic. $\text{ThFlat}$ gives up arbitrary nesting in exchange for a migration operator so simple it's nearly free to compute.

Each protocol declares its own instance theory, and the engine adapts. SQL gets `restrict`. ATProto gets tree surgery. Protobuf gets field projection. The outer framework (schema morphisms, existence checks, breaking-change detection) works uniformly across all three.


## Building a Protocol from Theories

Here is how a protocol is defined in panproto's TypeScript API:

```{.typescript include="../code/ts/10-custom-protocol.ts" snippet="define-protocol"}
```

A protocol is a record with two fields: a schema theory and an instance theory. The engine reads the theory structure (what sorts exist, what operations connect them, what axioms constrain them) and derives:

- **The migration type**: what data a migration must carry (vertex map, edge map, constraint map, ...).
- **The restriction operator**: how to apply a migration to an instance (`restrict`, tree surgery, or field projection).
- **The existence checks**: which conditions a proposed migration must satisfy to be valid (@sec-when-migrations-break).
- **The breaking-change classifier**: which migrations are safe and which are breaking (@sec-breaking-changes).

::: {.callout-caution}
## Question

If you designed a protocol with $\text{ThHypergraph}$ for schemas and $\text{ThWType}$ for instances, what kind of data format would it describe? Can you think of a real-world format that fits?
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse=true}
## Answer

A format with multi-ary structural relationships (like SQL's compound foreign keys) but tree-shaped data instances (like JSON documents). This combination would describe something like a document database with compound keys and nested values. MongoDB comes close: its schemas involve multi-field indexes and compound shard keys (hyperedge structure), while its documents are nested JSON trees (W-type instances).
:::

The protocol table shows that most schema theories are assembled from smaller pieces. This is not an accident.

## Why Colimits for Schema Theories?

Most schema theories in @tbl-protocol-table are colimits of simpler theories. Real-world protocols are assembled from orthogonal concerns:

- **Graph structure**: vertices and directed edges ($\text{ThGraph}$).
- **Constraint structure**: invariants on vertex/edge data ($\text{ThConstraint}$).
- **Multiplicity structure**: arrays, optional fields, repeated edges ($\text{ThMulti}$).
- **Interface structure**: abstract types implemented by concrete ones ($\text{ThInterface}$).
- **Hyperedge structure**: edges connecting more than two vertices simultaneously ($\text{ThHyperEdge}$).
- **Metadata structure**: out-of-band annotation layers ($\text{ThMeta}$).

These concerns are largely independent. ATProto needs graphs, constraints, and multiplicity. GraphQL adds interfaces. SQL replaces graphs with hypergraphs (for compound foreign keys) and drops multiplicity. The colimit construction assembles exactly the combination you need, with the categorical machinery ensuring the pieces fit together coherently.^[The colimit is taken in the category of algebraic theories. It identifies shared sorts and operations across the component theories, producing a theory that is simultaneously a model of each component. See @barr1990 for the general construction.]

ATProto's schema theory is $\mathrm{colimit}(\text{ThGraph}, \text{ThConstraint}, \text{ThMulti})$: a structure that is simultaneously a graph, a constrained structure, and a multiplicity-aware structure. SQL's is $\mathrm{colimit}(\text{ThHypergraph}, \text{ThConstraint})$. GraphQL's is $\mathrm{colimit}(\text{ThGraph}, \text{ThConstraint}, \text{ThMulti}, \text{ThInterface})$.

Group E protocols (XML/XSD, HTML, Neo4j) add $\text{ThMeta}$ to the constrained multigraph base. This captures the fact that these formats carry an additional annotation layer (XML attributes, HTML metadata, Neo4j property maps) alongside the tree structure. The instance theory correspondingly becomes $\mathrm{colimit}(\text{ThWType}, \text{ThMeta})$: a wellfounded tree plus a metadata layer that annotates nodes. Migration runs the standard W-type pipeline on the tree and a separate restriction pass on the metadata, with the two passes coupled at shared nodes.

The pattern is modular. If a new protocol needs graph structure, constraints, and some novel "versioning" concern, you write $\text{ThVersioning}$ as a new theory and take the colimit with existing pieces. The engine derives migration, existence checks, and breaking-change detection for the new combination without protocol-specific code.


## The Restrict Column

The instance theory does more than describe data shape; it determines which migration operator the engine uses.

The rightmost column of @tbl-protocol-table names the operation that applies a migration to data. This column is *determined by* the instance theory.

**`restrict` (precomposition).** When $\mathbb{T}_I = \text{ThFunctor}$, the restriction operator is `restrict`. Given a schema morphism $f: S_1 \to S_2$ and an instance $I$ over $S_2$, the restricted instance $f^* I$ looks up each old table in the new schema and pulls back its rows.

**Signature restriction + BFS + contraction.** When $\mathbb{T}_I = \text{ThWType}$, the restriction operator is the five-step tree surgery pipeline described in @sec-wtype-instances. Every step is necessary: skip reachability and you keep orphaned subtrees; skip contraction and you lose data that should survive; skip resolution and you get ambiguous edge labels.

**Field projection.** When $\mathbb{T}_I = \text{ThFlat}$, the restriction operator reduces to keeping mapped fields and dropping the rest. The BFS is trivial (root plus leaves only), contraction never fires, and fan reconstruction is a simple list filter. All Group C protocols (Protobuf, Avro, Thrift, Cap'n Proto, FlatBuffers, ASN.1, Bond, Redis, HCL) use this operator.


## From Table to Engine

The protocol table is not just documentation. It is a *specification* that the engine reads at startup. When you instantiate panproto for a particular protocol, the engine:

1. Parses $\mathbb{T}_S$ to determine what sorts ($\text{Vertex}$, $\text{Edge}$, $\text{Constraint}$, ...) exist and what operations connect them.
2. Parses $\mathbb{T}_I$ to determine the data representation (set-valued map, W-type, or flat).
3. Derives the migration type from $\mathbb{T}_S$'s sorts and operations. A migration must carry a map for each sort.
4. Derives the restriction operator from $\mathbb{T}_I$.
5. Derives existence checks from $\mathbb{T}_S$'s axioms (@sec-when-migrations-break).
6. Derives the breaking-change classifier from the interaction of $\mathbb{T}_S$ and $\mathbb{T}_I$ (@sec-breaking-changes).

Steps 3 through 6 are fully automatic. The protocol author provides the two theories; the engine provides the rest.


## Protocols as a Design Space

The two-parameter architecture organizes all 76 protocols into a *design space*. Each point is a $(\mathbb{T}_S, \mathbb{T}_I)$ pair; the six theory groups correspond to six clusters.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-design-space
%%| fig-cap: "The protocol design space. Horizontal axis: schema theory complexity. Vertical axis: instance theory family."
graph LR
    subgraph "Set-Valued Map Instances"
        B["Group B<br/>Hypergraph + Set-Valued Map<br/>(11 protocols)<br/>SQL, Parquet, Arrow, ..."]
    end
    subgraph "W-Type Instances"
        A["Group A<br/>Constrained Multigraph + W-Type<br/>(30 protocols)<br/>ATProto, JSON Schema, OpenAPI, ..."]
        D["Group D<br/>Typed Graph + W-Type + Interfaces<br/>(16 protocols)<br/>GraphQL, TypeScript, Rust Serde, ..."]
        E["Group E<br/>Constrained Multigraph + W-Type + Meta<br/>(9 protocols)<br/>XML/XSD, HTML, Neo4j, ..."]
    end
    subgraph "Flat Instances"
        C["Group C<br/>Simple Graph + Flat<br/>(9 protocols)<br/>Protobuf, Avro, Thrift, ..."]
    end
```

Some natural questions this framing surfaces:

- **What separates Group A from Group D?** Both use $\text{ThWType}$ instances over constrained multigraph schemas. Group D adds $\text{ThInterface}$: abstract types (interfaces, traits, type classes) as first-class schema citizens.
- **What makes Group E distinct from Group A?** Group E adds $\text{ThMeta}$ to the schema colimit and lifts the instance theory to $\mathrm{colimit}(\text{ThWType}, \text{ThMeta})$. The metadata layer participates in migration.
- **What lives between Group B and Group A?** A protocol with hypergraph schemas but W-type instances (perhaps a document database with compound keys and nested values). panproto can accommodate this by combining $\text{ThHypergraph}$, $\text{ThConstraint}$, and $\text{ThWType}$.

The design space is open.


## Historical Context

The idea that data migration can be parameterized by algebraic structure has roots in several traditions.

@spivak2012 showed that migration via structure-preserving maps between schema categories gives a clean, compositional account of SQL-style migration. The CQL project [@schultz2017] turned this into a practical tool for relational databases.

The W-type framework comes from type theory, where @gambino2004 showed that tree-shaped data structures arise naturally as initial algebras (the simplest data types that can represent arbitrary recursion). @abbott2005 connected this to "containers," a way of describing data shapes that panproto uses to determine how a schema's structure constrains the tree shape of its instances.

The insight that these traditions can be unified (that set-valued map instances, W-type instances, and flat instances all arise as models of different instance theories, with migration derived from the theory structure, and that 76 real-world protocols fall cleanly into six theory groups under this classification) is, as far as we know, original to panproto.

We now have the tools to walk through a concrete migration.
